title: Add 10 Using Place Value
description: Add 10 to a number by increasing the tens digit by 1 when no regrouping is needed.
condition: Expression of the form n + 10 where n is a three-digit number and no regrouping is required.
response: Increase the tens digit of n by 1; keep hundreds and ones unchanged.
label: Principle / Model

title: Add 100 Using Place Value
description: Add 100 to a three-digit number by increasing the hundreds digit by 1 while keeping tens and ones the same.
condition: Expression of the form n + 100 where n is a three-digit number and no regrouping is required.
response: Increase the hundreds digit of n by 1; keep tens and ones unchanged.
label: Principle / Model

title: Compare Hundreds Digits First
description: When comparing three-digit numbers, compare the hundreds digits to determine which number is greater if they differ.
condition: Two three-digit numbers with different hundreds digits
response: The number with the greater hundreds digit is greater
label: Principle / Model
example: Compare $796$ and $709$. Since $7=7$ in the hundreds place, compare tens (next step). But compare $796$ and $679$: since $7>6$ in the hundreds place, $796>679$.
notes: Applies before comparing tens or ones.

title: Compare Tens and Ones When Hundreds Are Equal
description: If two three-digit numbers have the same hundreds digit, compare tens digits; if tens are equal, compare ones digits.
condition: Two three-digit numbers with equal hundreds digits
response: Compare tens digits; if equal, compare ones digits to determine which number is greater
label: Principle / Model

title: Compare Three-Digit Numbers by Place Value
description: Determine which of two three-digit numbers is greater by comparing hundreds, then tens, then ones.
condition: Two three-digit numbers written in standard form.
response: Compare digits from left to right and determine <, >, or = based on the first place value that differs.
label: Principle / Model

title: Compose 1 Hundred from 10 Tens
description: Recognize that 10 tens can be regrouped as 1 hundred.
condition: A representation includes 10 tens.
response: Replace 10 tens with 1 hundred.
label: Concept

title: Convert Expanded Form to Standard Form
description: Combine hundreds, tens, and ones written in expanded form into a single standard-form number.
condition: A number written as a sum of hundreds, tens, and ones (e.g., 300 + 40 + 3).
response: Add the place-value parts to write the standard-form number.
label: Principle / Model

title: Count Base-Ten Blocks
description: Determine the total value represented by a combination of hundreds, tens, and ones blocks.
condition: Given any combination of hundreds flats, tens rods, and ones cubes.
response: Calculate total value by summing (hundreds × 100) + (tens × 10) + ones.
label: Principle

title: Evaluate Sum of Three-Digit Expressions
description: Compute the value of an expression involving addition of three-digit numbers or place-value parts.
condition: An addition expression such as 4 + 80 + 200 or 118 + 10 + 10.
response: Add the quantities to find the total.
label: Principle / Model

title: Identify Hundred Block
description: Recognize that a 10×10 flat represents 100.
condition: Given a single 10×10 base-ten flat.
response: State that its value is 100.
label: Fact

title: Identify Ones Cube
description: Recognize that a single small cube represents 1.
condition: Given a single unit cube.
response: State that its value is 1.
label: Fact

title: Identify Ten Rod
description: Recognize that a base-ten rod represents 10.
condition: Given a single base-ten rod.
response: State that its value is 10.
label: Fact

title: Interpret Less-Than Symbol
description: Understand that the symbol < means the number on the left is less than the number on the right.
condition: An inequality statement using the symbol <
response: Interpret the statement as left number is less than right number
label: Fact

title: Label Consecutive Integers on Unit Number Line
description: When a number line shows consecutive whole numbers with unit spacing, label each tick mark by counting by ones.
condition: A number line with endpoints given and evenly spaced tick marks representing increments of 1
response: Assign consecutive whole numbers increasing by 1 from left endpoint to right endpoint
label: Principle / Model
example: If the left endpoint is $380$ and each tick mark increases by $1$, the labels are $380,381,382,\dots,390$.
notes: Requires recognizing total difference and equal spacing.

title: Match Representation to Target Number
description: Determine whether a base-ten representation equals a specified target number.
condition: Given a target number (e.g., 200) and a base-ten block representation.
response: Select or confirm the representations whose total value equals the target.
label: Principle
example: If the target is 200 and the model shows 2 hundreds, then 2(100) = 200, so it matches.

title: Order Numbers from Greatest to Least
description: Arrange a set of numbers in descending order based on place value comparison.
condition: A list of three-digit numbers with instruction to order from greatest to least
response: Sequence the numbers from largest value to smallest value
label: Principle / Model
example: Given $697, 769, 679, 709, 796$, ordering greatest to least gives $796, 769, 709, 697, 679$.
notes: Builds on place value comparison KCs.

title: Recognize Equivalent Place Value Representations
description: Identify different representations that equal the same three-digit number.
condition: Multiple expressions or descriptions of hundreds, tens, and ones.
response: Determine which representations equal the given number.
label: Principle / Model

title: Select Digit to Make Inequality True
description: Determine which digit in a missing place makes a three-digit number satisfy a given inequality.
condition: A three-digit number with one unknown digit compared to another number using < or >
response: Choose digit(s) that make the inequality true based on place value comparison
label: Principle / Model
example: For $8\_9 < 821$, compare hundreds (8=8). Compare tens: to be less than $821$, the tens digit must be $0$ or $1$, giving $809$ or $819$.

title: Skip Count by Tens on a Number Line
description: Label equally spaced tick marks on a number line by counting by tens starting at 0.
condition: A number line starting at 0 where each interval represents 10.
response: Label successive tick marks as 0, 10, 20, 30, ...
label: Principle / Model

title: Write Expanded Form (Three-Digit Number)
description: Express a three-digit number as the sum of its hundreds, tens, and ones values.
condition: Given any three-digit number.
response: Write it as (hundreds × 100) + (tens × 10) + ones.
label: Principle

title: Write Standard Numeral from Base-Ten Representation
description: Translate a base-ten block representation into a three-digit numeral using place value.
condition: Given counts of hundreds, tens, and ones blocks.
response: Write the corresponding three-digit number with digits matching each place value.
label: Principle

title: Write Three-Digit Number in Words
description: Convert a three-digit numeral into its standard written word form.
condition: Given any three-digit numeral.
response: Write the number using words following standard place-value naming conventions.
label: Rule / Plan
notes: Conventions (e.g., hyphenation) may vary slightly by curriculum.
